project_overview.project_name: South Sudan Energy Sector Access and Institutional Strengthening Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: To increase access to electricity services and strengthen the institutional capacity of the electricity sector in South Sudan.
project_overview.total_financing: US$53 million (US$50 million IDA Grant + US$3 million Japan PHRD Fund)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 340,000 people in Juba and Yei through grid connections; 1.2 million people served by 50 health institutions; 750,000 people in host communities and refugee camps; energy sector institutions (MoED, SSEC)

primary_locations: Greater Upper Nile Region (Upper Nile, Jonglei, Unity states)
geographic_scope.coverage_type: national with focus on specific urban centers and health institutions
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - South Sudan is classified as fragile and conflict-affected. Project specifically targets refugee-hosting areas and host communities. Approximately 308,374 refugees hosted (as of December 2022), with majority in Jamjang and Bunj. Project includes US$20 million from Window for Host Communities and Refugees (WHR).

key_components.component_name: Grid densification and extension in Juba and vicinity areas
key_components.description: 40,000 new connections (20,000 residential/SME under existing network; 20,000 through MV/LV network expansion). Includes 11,000 LED streetlights. Climate-resilient design with 7-meter ground clearance and concrete poles for flood-prone areas.

Solar plus battery hybridization and rehabilitation of existing isolated grid in Yei. 3 MW solar + 12 MWh battery storage + 1.2 MW diesel backup. Target: 10,000 households and 400 commercial/institutional customers. Operating concession model with private operator. Feasibility studies for other state capitals.

key_components.component_name: Off-grid electrification of health and selected public institutions through standalone solar systems
key_components.description: Solar and battery-based off-grid solutions for ~50 health institutions (Payam-level hospitals and PHCCs) and education institutions. Focus on CEmONC services. Implementation through UNICEF partnership. Includes O&M sustainability mechanisms.
key_components.financing: US$10 million (US$7 million IDA + US$3 million PHRD)

key_components.description: Subcomponents: (4.1) Legal/regulatory framework development; (4.2) Sector planning and capacity building; (4.3) SSEC strengthening; (4.4) Off-grid solar market development; (4.5) Gender gap narrowing; (4.6) PIU support and capacity building. Includes owner's engineer recruitment and institutional strengthening.
Flexible component to support GoSS response to eligible emergencies. Capitalized by drawing on unused funds from Components 1-4. Governed by World Bank Policy on Investment Project Financing.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Energy and Dams (MoED) as lead implementing agency. Project Implementation Unit (PIU) established within MoED with secondees from SSEC, JEDCO, and MoH. UNICEF implements Component 3 (health institution electrification) under Output Agreement.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) chaired by Minister of MoED, including core members from MoFP and SSEC Chairperson. Observer organizations: JEDCO, Ministry of Health, Ministry of Education, state government representatives, UNICEF, UNHCR, Commission for Refugee Affairs (CRA), Embassy of Japan. PSC meets quarterly.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (July 2016, revised November 2020). Competitive procurement for EPC contractors. Prior review for high-risk/high-value contracts; post review for others. Use of World Bank Standard Procurement Documents. All contracts tracked through STEP system. Procurement Committee established at MoED. Forced labor declarations required for solar panel suppliers.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Quarterly reports from MoED on PDO and intermediate indicators. M&E framework includes sex-disaggregated indicators. Midterm review at project midpoint. End-of-term review and Implementation Completion and Results Report at closure. UNICEF reports annually on Component 3. Third-party verification of UNICEF performance. Geo-Enabling Initiative for Monitoring and Supervision (GEMS) technology used for real-time monitoring.

identified_risks.risk_description: National election scheduled for December 2024 when transitional government mandate expires. High risk of political instability and conflict hampering implementation. Potential civil unrest and political tensions.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design remains agile and adaptive. Pre-selected third-party implementer can be contracted if government implementation becomes infeasible. Close monitoring with World Bank, local communities, international organizations, and diplomatic communities. Component 3 can shift to third-party implementation if needed. Political risks related to refugee issues assessed as minimal due to government's generous refugee policy.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: MoED and SSEC lack technical capacity for project implementation and sector planning. Limited experience with World Bank-financed projects (ESTAP cancelled in 2015 due to conflict). Chronic underpayment of civil servants leading to brain drain and low morale. Weak institutional capacity to maintain assets and conduct systematic planning.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Establishment of PIU with qualified consultants and specialists. Owner's engineer hired to support technical implementation. Intensive capacity building under Component 4 including training on planning, procurement, FM, and E&S standards. In-country World Bank staff and local consultants for policy dialogue. Secondees from SSEC and other agencies to PIU. Twinning arrangements with consultants. Continuous training and capacity development programs.

identified_risks.risk_description: High corruption levels (South Sudan ranks 179 out of 180 on Transparency International index). Weak regulatory environment and underdeveloped financial sector. Chronic underpayment of civil servants creates incentives for corruption. Weak internal controls and audit functions. Limited procurement capacity and non-operational procurement system.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: FM specialist and project accountant hired for PIU. Segregation of duties in payment processing. Internal audit unit established. Training provided to FM and procurement teams. Designated Account (DA) in commercial bank with ringfenced project funds. Transaction-based Statement of Expenditure (SOE) disbursement method. Direct payment method for consultants/contractors. Monthly and quarterly financial reporting to World Bank. External audits by private CPA firms. Procurement Committee oversight. STEP system for transparency. Hands-on Enhanced Implementation Support (HEIS) considered. Third-party monitoring agencies used selectively.

identified_risks.risk_description: Volatile macroeconomic situation with sustained depreciation of South Sudanese pound. Rising global inflation and commodity prices. Supply chain disruptions increasing project costs (solar panels/accessories increased 20-30% between 2021-2022). Most electricity costs denominated in hard currency. Fiscal deficits and budget constraints.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Contingency budget included in project costing. Technical assistance on electricity tariff framework. Government macro-fiscal reforms (exchange rate unification, inflation control) provide some mitigation. Monitoring of global commodity prices and supply chains.

identified_risks.risk_description: Most key energy sector policy, planning, and legal documents are outdated, fragmented, or non-existent. National Electricity Bill drafted in 2016 contains gaps and awaits revisions. No formalized sector master plans. Weak institutional capacity for systematic planning. Conflicting institutional mandates between MoED and SSEC.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Component 4 dedicated to legal, regulatory, and sector planning work. Support for refinement of 2016 Electricity Bill and complementary legislation. Development of grid code, tariff setting mechanism, licensing standards, and PPP framework. Electricity sector planning with focus on near-to-medium term scenarios. Capacity building for MoED and SSEC staff on planning tools and methodologies.

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.risk_description: Some technical arrangements including specific pilot locations (Component 2) and detailed costing may change during implementation. Conceptual design for mini-grids and health institutions components may require refinement.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Owner's Engineer recruited to provide technical support during implementation. PIU staffed with qualified engineers and technical experts. Continued technical coordination and monitoring. Detailed design finalized before contractor engagement. Site visits and technical assessments conducted.

identified_risks.risk_description: Generation of hazardous and non-hazardous wastes including e-waste from batteries and solar panels. Potential soil erosion, sedimentation, and landscape disturbance. Risks to flora and fauna from vegetation clearance and fuel spillage. Health and safety risks to workers and communities. Temporary loss of livelihoods (street vendors in Juba). Influx of migrant workers and social tensions. GBV and violence against children in fragile environment. Forced labor risks in solar panel supply chain. Land acquisition impacts.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) prepared and disclosed. Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) outlines compliance requirements. Site-specific Environmental and Social Management Plans (ESMPs) to be prepared before sub-project activities. Security Risk Management Plan developed. Social Assessment and Resettlement Policy Framework prepared. Labor Management Procedure addressing child and forced labor. GBV Action Plan and GBV risk assessment. Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) with consultation strategy. Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) with multiple uptake channels. Forced labor bidder declarations and enhanced contractual provisions. Climate-resilient design with flood risk assessment. Training on GBV prevention and response. Community sensitization on GBV and violence against children.

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak inter-ministerial coordination and political interactions in South Sudan. Inadequate engagement with regional and local authorities. Fragmented and conflicting institutional mandates in power sector. Weak stakeholder coordination mechanisms.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project Steering Committee structure designed for close and systematic coordination among sector institutions and stakeholders. Observer group including UNHCR, state governments, and other ministries. Transparent and organized stakeholder engagement mechanisms. Coordination with CRA and UNHCR for refugee-related activities. Regular PSC meetings (quarterly). Technical coordination through PIU with secondees from relevant agencies.

identified_risks.risk_description: Remoteness of project sites and scattered implementation locations pose challenges for coordination, contract management, and monitoring. Limited accessibility during rainy season (only 6-month dry season window). Difficult security situation in some areas limits intervention feasibility. Risks to project staff and contractors.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Yei selected as pilot mini-grid site due to relatively favorable security situation and proximity to Juba. Geospatial analysis used to identify accessible and secure intervention areas. Proactive engagement of implementation functionaries and stakeholders. Contract Implementation Plans for monitoring. Web-based application for contract monitoring. Security Risk Management Plan. Careful site selection based on security assessments.

triggered_safeguard_policies: Environmental and Social Standards (ESS) 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS 7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities
OP 7.50: Projects on International Waterways (exception to notification requirement approved)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) established with multiple uptake channels: (1) Grievance hotline operator phone number widely disseminated; (2) Help desk at project locality manned by PIU staff in coordination with local authorities; (3) Anonymous suggestion boxes at nearest Boma or Payam office; (4) Community meetings and public hearings. GRM accessible to refugee and host community populations. Different submission methods for GBV/SEA/SH-related grievances. All personnel receiving grievances trained in handling sensitive cases. Feedback mechanism receives, registers, and addresses suggestions, concerns, and grievances in safe and confidential manner. World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Accountability Mechanism also available.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Strong gender focus with five-year gender work plan to be adopted before project effectiveness. Gender Gap Assessment (GGA) conducted identifying significant disparities: only 1 female technical staff out of 21 at MoED; only 1 female technical staff out of 41 at SSEC; all leadership positions held by men; low female representation in STEM fields. Subcomponent 4.5 dedicated to narrowing gender gap. Activities include: establishment of gender units in HR departments of MoED and SSEC; appointment of gender focal persons (woman and man); development of gender-responsive HR policies with equal wage provisions; adoption of gender equality and GBV/SEA/SH prevention policies; capacity development programs for female staff; STEM career counselling for girls in schools; male engagement and gender equality champions. Three gender-related intermediate indicators in Results Framework. Component 3 prioritizes CEmONC services to benefit women and girls. Gender specialist hired in PIU.
vulnerable_groups: Refugees (308,374 as of December 2022, majority in Jamjang and Bunj)
Women and girls (particularly young mothers, pregnant women)
Children (including risk of child labor and violence)
Street vendors and informal workers (temporary livelihood displacement in Juba)
Remote rural populations with limited access to services

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity. South Sudan classified as Fragile and Conflict-Affected Situation (FCS). Project components aligned with Global Crisis Response Framework (GCRF): Component 1 tagged to GCRF Pillar 4 (Resilient construction, green and sustainable growth); Component 2 tagged to GCRF Pillar 2 (Special support for refugees/IDPs) and Pillar 4; Component 3 tagged to GCRF Pillar 2 (Routine health services, special support for refugees/IDPs) and Pillar 3 (Crisis preparedness, adaptive social protection); Component 4 tagged to GCRF Pillar 4 (Climate-smart policies, institutional strengthening). Project includes US$20 million from Window for Host Communities and Refugees (WHR). Strong coordination with UNHCR and Commission for Refugee Affairs (CRA). Electrification of health institutions supports conflict-affected populations' access to essential services. Off-grid solutions reduce dependence on diesel generators and associated security risks. Project designed to be resilient to political instability with contingency arrangements for third-party implementation if government-led approach becomes infeasible.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: South Sudan remains caught in vicious cycle of fragility, conflict, and violence (FCV). Key fragility factors: (1) Recent conflict history - civil war 2013-2018, followed by fragile peace under R-ARCSS (September 2018); (2) Transitional government established February 2020 with election scheduled December 2024 - high risk of renewed instability; (3) Elevated violence in several parts of country despite national-level ceasefire; (4) Massive displacement - 308,374 refugees hosted plus significant IDP populations; (5) Extreme poverty - 80% poverty rate (2022), up from 77% (2020); (6) Food insecurity - 63% of population (7.8 million) facing acute emergency or catastrophic food insecurity (November 2022); (7) Climate shocks - worst floods since 1960s in 2021 affecting 760,000 people; record-breaking 2022 floods affecting 1 million people; (8) Economic stagnation - GDP contraction 5% (2021) and 2.3% (2022); (9) Weak institutions - ranked last (191st) on Human Development Index 2021; (10) Limited basic service delivery - only 5.3% electricity access (lowest in Sub-Saharan Africa); (11) Opaque public financial management and widespread corruption; (12) Chronic underpayment of civil servants causing brain drain and low institutional capacity.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project includes Security Risk Management Plan. Security situation varies by location - Yei selected for mini-grid pilot due to relatively favorable security situation and proximity to Juba. Other potential intervention areas face security constraints limiting feasibility. Project design includes flexibility to shift implementation modality if security deteriorates. Component 1 includes 11,000 LED streetlights to improve security in Juba and create safer environment for businesses and residents. Project staff and contractors face security risks requiring careful management. Coordination with local authorities and security forces necessary. Monitoring and supervision missions require security clearances and risk assessments. Project remains adaptive to changing security conditions.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Significant displacement and refugee populations are integral to project design. South Sudan hosts 308,374 refugees (as of December 31, 2022) with 64,225 refugee households. Over 90% located in two areas: Jamjang (Pariang County, Ruweng Administrative Area) and Bunj Town (Maban County, Upper Nile State). Government maintains open-door refugee policy and reaffirmed commitment to address vulnerabilities. Refugee Act of 2012 provides legal framework aligned with international standards. Refugees granted freedom of movement, access to land for cultivation and livelihoods, and practical reception/registration arrangements. Project includes US$20 million from Window for Host Communities and Refugees (WHR) specifically to benefit refugee and host communities. Component 1 targets 340,000 people in Juba and Yei including refugees and host communities. Component 2 (Yei mini-grid) specifically includes refugee and host community populations in target beneficiaries. Component 3 (health institutions) prioritizes institutions in refugee-hosting areas including Jamjang and Maban. Approximately 750,000 people in host communities (453,232) and refugee camps (297,024) expected to benefit from improved electricity access and security lighting. Project coordinates with UNHCR and Commission for Refugee Affairs (CRA) for implementation. Stakeholder engagement mechanisms designed to be accessible to refugee and host community populations. GRM adapted for refugee populations. Project addresses unique energy access challenges of displaced populations including loss of personal energy assets and competition for natural resources.
